Notation: F, F_t, and the fZ_X.Y schema

Source: vignettes/notation.Rmd
notation.Rmd

This vignette pins down the notation used by every other piece of the package: how a functional field is written, how its parameters are labelled, and how those labels map onto lavaan model strings. It is short on prose and long on conventions — the goal is a fixed target that other vignettes and helpers can refer back to without re-deriving.

Two objects with the same letter

A functional field appears in two forms:

  • F — the time-invariant field. A square matrix of equations, indexed F[X, Y] = the force from source X to target Y. Each cell holds an expression in the situational state s; in practice those expressions are simple linear combinations of state variables, but in principle they can be arbitrary.

  • F_t = F(s_t) — the time-varying field. The numerical 2-matrix obtained by evaluating each cell of F at the current state s_t.

The Markov step has two sub-steps, both keyed on s_t:

Ft=F(st)st+1=st⋅Ft. F_t = F(s_t) \qquad s_{t+1} = s_t \cdot F_t.

F is fixed; F_t changes from step to step because s_t does. Actions enter the loop by perturbing s_t (s_t[i] \leftarrow s_t[i] + \delta). Iteration continues until the field stops producing change, at which point a designated selection signal L_s (typically rated likelihood in ESJT data) is read off.

The source-then-target indexing (F[X, Y]) is deliberate and contrasts with the standard SEM B[Y, X] convention. Anywhere a coefficient is extracted from lavaan and exposed as an F-cell, it is transposed at the boundary.

The parameter schema

Every estimable parameter in F carries a label of the form

𝚏𝚉_𝚇.𝚈 \boxed{\texttt{fZ\_X.Y}}

Read as: the f-convention coefficient on the force from X to Y, under moderator Z. The schema is universal — every parameter fits this template — and is regular in two ways:

  1. The baseline (unmoderated) coefficient is the special case Z = 1, written f1_X.Y. It is the “interaction of X with the constant 1” — i.e., the part of the force that fires regardless of the situation. We keep the 1 explicit; there is no bare f_X.Y.
  2. Multiple moderators on the same cell are written as multiple fZ_X.Y terms with different Z. There is no built-in limit on how many a cell can carry.

The separators are load-bearing: _ separates the moderator from the path, and . separates source from target within the path. Both _ and . are valid R identifier characters, so lavaan parses the labels intact (verified against lavaan 0.6-21).

Generic single-letter slots drop the separator. When the source and target are the one-letter schematic roles X, M, Y — as they always are on the inductive side, where pathXMY() fits a generic X → M → Y template — the . is unnecessary and the keys are written without it: f1_XM, fZ_MY, fZ_XY (read the two trailing letters as source then target). The dotted form is reserved for named nodes, as on the deductive side (fS2_TurnOn.HCoPot), where multi-character names would otherwise run together. Both forms render to the same matrix-cell display, F[src,tgt] — fZ_XM and FZ[X,M] are the same parameter, one a lavaan-safe key, the other a reading aid (see The F1 / FZ matrix overlay and pathXMY_to_F()).

Reading a cell

A cell F[X, Y] with a baseline term and one Z-moderation expands as

F[X,Y]=f1_X.Y+fZ_X.Y⋅s[Z]. F[X, Y] = f1\_X.Y \;+\; fZ\_X.Y \cdot s[Z].

Evaluated at s_t:

Ft[X,Y]=f1_X.Y+fZ_X.Y⋅st[Z]. F_t[X, Y] = f1\_X.Y \;+\; fZ\_X.Y \cdot s_t[Z].

Read into the propagation step: the contribution of source X to target Y at time t is s_t[X] · F_t[X, Y]. So Y’s update, summing over all sources, is

st+1[Y]=∑Xst[X]⋅Ft[X,Y]. s_{t+1}[Y] \;=\; \sum_X s_t[X] \cdot F_t[X, Y].

Lavaan-syntax mapping

A cell F[X, Y] with a baseline term and a Z-moderation term becomes one row in a lavaan model:

Y ~ f1_X.Y * X + fZ_X.Y * Z:X

The target sits on the left of ~ (lavaan’s regression convention); the source sits on the right; the moderator enters as the leading member of a Z:X interaction. The interaction is written condition first, action last so its order matches the label: Z:X ↔︎ fZ_X.Y. This is load-bearing for labelF(), which auto-generates the labels by reading the final right-hand variable as the source/action and any leading ones as the condition. The parameter labels record the F-convention semantics regardless of lavaan’s left/right orientation.

A small worked check — the model parses cleanly and the labels survive lavaanify():

library(lavaan)
#> This is lavaan 0.6-21
#> lavaan is FREE software! Please report any bugs.
mod <- "Y ~ f1_X.Y * X + fZ_X.Y * Z:X"
pt  <- lavaanify(mod, fixed.x = FALSE)
pt[pt$op == "~", c("lhs", "op", "rhs", "label")]
#>   lhs op rhs  label
#> 1   Y  ~   X f1_X.Y
#> 2   Y  ~ Z:X fZ_X.Y

A cell with only a Z-moderation term and no baseline is an afforded force: the source X contributes nothing to Y when s[Z] = 0, and the force becomes available once s[Z] flips on. This is how the redesigned coffee vignette expresses prerequisites-must-hold actions:

HCoPot ~ fS2_TurnOn.HCoPot * s2:TurnOn

The TurnOn → HCoPot force is zero until s2 is flipped on by an earlier action. The Markov iteration handles the rest.

Situation, action plan, and fixed coefficients

The forces above describe the situation — the affordance structure of the world, independent of any agent. Layered on top is an action plan: conditional commitments of the form “fire action A once state Z is achieved”, written as state → action forces:

make   ~ 1 * s1
TurnOn ~ 1 * s2

Two notational points. First, plan forces run opposite to situation forces — state → action, where the situation runs action → outcome. Second, the coefficient is fixed (1 * s1, lavaan’s fixed-coefficient syntax) rather than carrying an fZ_X.Y label: a plan trigger is a structural commitment, not a quantity estimated from data, so it lives outside the schema and needs no params entry. evalF() values a fixed term straight from its coefficient.

Because both halves are plain lavaan strings, the field is assembled by concatenation — paste(situation, plan) — and run as one model. By visualization convention the plan’s forces are drawn in gold (plotField(..., plan = plan)), marking the forces a conditional action plan adds on top of the situation. Splitting the field this way takes about twice as many iterations to settle (each stage is now two hops, state → action → outcome), but reaches the same fixed point as injecting the actions on an external schedule.

The F1 / FZ matrix overlay

A field’s parameters can be reorganized into a stack of per-moderator matrices:

  • F1: cell (i, j) = f1_i.j — the baseline force matrix.
  • FZ: cell (i, j) = fZ_i.j — the contribution to F that is proportional to s[Z].
  • FZ', FZ'', … — one matrix per additional moderator.

Each matrix is itself an indexable view onto the underlying parameter table. Visualization overlays them — for example, render F1 as the black edges that are always active, and FZ as colored edges that strengthen with s[Z]. The three-panel decomposition figures in the speeding pathXMY vignette are an early hand-built instance of this; generalising the data side via pathXMY_to_F() lets the same visualization machinery render whole fields rather than single triangles.

The matrix view is also the convenient handoff to the runtime: a step s_{t+1} = s_t \cdot F_t decomposes as

Ft=F1+∑Zst[Z]⋅FZ, F_t \;=\; F_1 \;+\; \sum_Z s_t[Z] \cdot F_Z,

so F_t is a state-weighted sum of the overlay matrices. This is exactly the form evalF() consumes.

What the schema does not impose

The label convention is purely a naming discipline. It does not by itself commit any cell to a particular functional form:

  • A cell can be a constant (f1_X.Y only) — a topological edge, fit by an ordinary path coefficient.
  • A cell can be state-linear (f1_X.Y + one or more fZ_X.Y terms) — a moderated edge, fit by regression with interactions.
  • A cell can be an afforded force (one fZ_X.Y term, no f1_X.Y) — fires only when s[Z] > 0. The structural-masking trick the coffee vignette relies on.
  • A cell can carry multiple moderators simultaneously by stacking fZ_X.Y terms; the linear-combination interpretation extends.

Nonlinearities like s[a] \cdot s[b] gates appear as a:b:X interaction terms in the lavaan-syntax mapping (conditions first, action last), with a label of the form fab_X.Y (compound moderator). The package treats compound moderators as one logical “Z” — the matrix-overlay view holds an extra F_{ab} matrix; nothing else changes.

f1_X.Y as a placeholder

Read strictly, every cell of F is afforded: there are no truly unconditional forces. When f1_X.Y appears in a model, it captures the X → Y force under whatever conditions held during the relevant data or theory — without naming those conditions. The “1” is a shorthand for “a state variable that happens to be true throughout the data we have.” So a f1_X.Y term is best read as a frank acknowledgement that a force is present in these conditions, and that the conditions that gate it have not yet been identified.

A mature representation of a process replaces every f1_X.Y with one or more fZ_X.Y for the Z’s that determine when the force fires — and which, in principle, can be intervened on to interrupt it. The schema admits f1_X.Y as a convenience (forcing full conditional specification at every iteration would stall any working model), but the conceptual direction of progress is to push every f1 toward an fZ decomposition over time. A discovered “main effect” is, on this reading, a moderation effect waiting for its moderator to be named.

Node-name conventions

Names that appear on either side of ~, or inside an X:Z interaction, must be lavaan-safe: letters, digits, and _ only. No parens, no ., no other punctuation. The . is reserved as the source/target separator inside parameter labels.

This is a change from the original coffee_field_model vignette, which used names like H(Co).Pot and make(s2).p. The redesigned vignette renames these to HCoPot and make_s2. The pretty display labels (with parens and dots) remain available for visualization — prepField() and the plotting helpers carry a separate display-label map.

The two roles a field can play

The same F lives at the centre of two complementary workflows:

Role Direction Tool Parameters
Induction — learn the world from observed behaviour Data → F pathXMY() and friends Fit, uncertain, carry SEs
Deduction — use a working theory of the world to plan F → behaviour runF(), chooseAction(), the redesigned coffee vignette Hand-set, treated as known

Both directions use the same model syntax and the same parameter schema. The bridge between them — turning a pathXMY fit into a runnable field — is pathXMY_to_F().

Migration from the legacy B1_MX / BZ_MX labels

Earlier versions of pathXMY() labelled their tidy_loop / tidy_joint tables in the SEM-style B1_MX / BZ_YX convention (prefix B, scope digit, target-source suffix). The tables now key on the F-schema generic-slot form directly — prefix f, scope digit, source-target suffix, no separator (single-letter slots). The bracket column is the display form produced by pathXMY_to_F():

Legacy label F-schema key Display Reads as
B1_MX f1_XM F1[X,M] baseline X → M force
BZ_MX fZ_XM FZ[X,M] Z-moderation of X → M
B1_YM f1_MY F1[M,Y] baseline M → Y force
BZ_YM fZ_MY FZ[M,Y] Z-moderation of M → Y
B1_YX f1_XY F1[X,Y] direct X → Y (controlling for M)
BZ_YX fZ_XY FZ[X,Y] Z-moderation of direct X → Y

The joint multi-mediator rows carry a _joint suffix (f1_XY_joint, fZ_XY_joint, …); the no-mediator total is written f1_XY with a * superscript in display, F1*[X,Y]. pathXMY_to_F() (see its own help page) renders any of these keys — singles, products (fZ_XM * f1_MY → FZ[X,M]·F1[M,Y]), and _joint / _total / (direct) variants — into the bracket display used throughout the pathXMY vignettes. The deductive engine keeps the dotted, named-node form fZ_X.Y (above); the two coincide once nodes are single-letter slots.
